items: Receptors are usually proteins that bind drugs and initiate biologic responses.

blocks.label: Main types
items: Regulatory proteins
Enzymes
Ion channels
Transport proteins
Receptors determine dose–response relationships.
Receptors determine selectivity.
Agonists activate receptors.
Antagonists bind without activating the receptor and reduce agonist effect.

blocks.label: Key terms
items: Kd = concentration at which 50% of receptors are occupied
EC50 = concentration causing 50% of maximal effect
Emax = maximal response

sections.title: Dose–Response Curves

items: Increasing dose increases response until plateau.
Log-dose plots create sigmoid curves for easier interpretation.

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 2-2 — Log dose axis & sigmoid dose–response

sections.title: Spare Receptors

items: Maximal response can occur before all receptors are occupied.
Increases tissue sensitivity.

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 2-1 — Drug concentration, effect & receptor occupancy

blocks.label: Competitive antagonist
items: Reversible
Shifts dose-response curve to the right
Emax unchanged

blocks.label: Noncompetitive antagonist
items: Irreversible or allosteric
Decreases Emax

sections.title: Partial Agonists

items: Produce a lower maximal response than a full agonist, even at full receptor occupancy.
Can behave as agonists when no full agonist is present, or functionally antagonize a full agonist by competing for receptors.

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 2-4 — Full vs partial agonist effects

items: Chemical antagonism: protamine vs heparin
Physiologic antagonism: insulin vs glucocorticoids

blocks.label: A. Intracellular receptors
items: Lipid-soluble drugs cross the cell membrane to reach cytoplasmic or nuclear receptors.
Drug–receptor complex acts as a transcription factor → alters gene expression.
Onset: slow (hours to days) — requires new protein synthesis.
Examples: steroids, thyroid hormone, vitamin D, retinoic acid.

blocks.label: B. Receptor tyrosine kinases
items: Ligand binds extracellular domain → activates intrinsic tyrosine kinase in cytoplasmic domain.
Autophosphorylation triggers downstream signaling cascade.
Involved in cell growth, differentiation, and metabolism.
Examples: insulin receptor, EGF receptor, PDGF receptor.

blocks.label: C. Cytokine receptors (JAK-STAT)
items: No intrinsic kinase activity — receptor associates with JAK (Janus kinase).
JAK phosphorylates STAT proteins → STAT translocates to nucleus → gene transcription.
Examples: interferons, interleukins, erythropoietin.
Exam pearl: JAK inhibitors (e.g., tofacitinib) block this pathway.

blocks.label: D. Ligand-gated ion channels
items: Ligand binds → conformational change → ion channel opens immediately.
Fastest signal transduction — onset in milliseconds.
Examples: nicotinic ACh receptor (Na⁺/K⁺), GABA-A receptor (Cl⁻).
Exam pearl: benzodiazepines increase frequency of Cl⁻ channel opening at GABA-A.

blocks.label: E. G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs)
items: Largest receptor superfamily (>800 genes in humans).
7 transmembrane domains; coupled to G proteins that activate second messengers.
Examples: β-adrenoceptors, muscarinic receptors, opioid receptors.
Gs → ↑ cAMP | Gi → ↓ cAMP | Gq → ↑ IP3/DAG/Ca²⁺

blocks.label: cAMP pathway
items: Activates protein kinase A

blocks.label: IP3/DAG pathway
items: IP3 increases intracellular calcium
DAG activates PKC

blocks.label: cGMP pathway
items: Smooth muscle relaxation
Nitric oxide signaling
blocks.note: No separate Ch.2 figure — remember: NO activates guanylyl cyclase → ↑ cGMP (e.g. vascular smooth muscle relaxation).

items: Desensitization
Downregulation
Upregulation

sections.title: Potency vs Efficacy

items: Potency = concentration or dose required for a given effect; lower EC50 means higher potency
Efficacy = maximal effect achievable, reflected by Emax

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 2-15 — Graded dose–response: potency vs efficacy

items: ED₅₀ = dose producing the desired effect in 50% of a population
TD₅₀ = dose producing toxicity in 50% of a population
LD₅₀ = dose lethal to 50% of animals in preclinical studies

blocks.content: Therapeutic index: TI = TD₅₀ / ED₅₀ — a larger TI indicates a wider safety margin.

items: Pharmacokinetics
Endogenous ligand levels
Receptor changes
Post-receptor changes
Drugs are selective, not specific.

blocks.label: Example
items: **Propranolol** — non-selective β blocker; worsens asthma by blocking β2 receptors.
**Verapamil** — Ca²⁺ channel blocker; lowers BP without bronchoconstriction.
**Metoprolol** — β1-selective blocker; safer alternative in asthmatic patients.
